topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. Liam was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but his fingers brushed against a cardboard box tucked beneath a moth-eaten tapestry. Inside, nestled amongst yellowed tissue paper, were stacks and stacks of meticulously organized schoolwork. Every assignment, every test, every essay from elementary school through high school—all accounted for. Liam blinked, the sudden weight of the discovery making his chest feel tight. He pulled out a crumpled worksheet from sophomore year history. His hands trembled slightly as he smoothed the paper, the neat, looping handwriting of his mother filling his vision. A strange, hollow sensation settled in his stomach, a dizzying sense of disorientation. He backed up a step, suddenly aware of the silence of the attic amplifying the frantic thumping in his ears.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Maya asked, her voice thin, holding a sheaf of neatly bound notebooks. Her mother, Sarah, merely smiled, a faint, knowing curve of her lips that didn't quite reach her eyes. Maya was supposed to be clearing out the garage, but she found a locked trunk in the back corner. When she finally forced the latch, the contents were staggering: years of schoolwork, perfectly cataloged by date and subject. Maya’s breath hitched. She picked up a geometry proof from middle school. The paper felt impossibly heavy. A wave of intense unease washed over her, making her grip tighten on the notebook until her knuckles were white. She looked up at her mother, searching for an explanation, but Sarah was already turning back to the window, her composure unnervingly perfect.

[story 3]

It was a Tuesday afternoon, and Alex was supposed to be helping his mother tidy the basement. Instead, he found a large, unmarked wooden crate filled floor-to-ceiling with paper. Inside, it was a chronological archive of his academic life. He started pulling things out, his movements jerky and hesitant. A stack of meticulously graded English papers caught his attention. As he flipped through them, a cold prickle ran down his spine. He stopped, his heart beginning to beat a rapid, uneven rhythm against his ribs. He felt a sudden urge to flee, to slam the lid shut and pretend none of this existed. The sheer volume of preserved effort felt overwhelming, a silent testament to a life he hadn't known existed. He dropped the papers back into the crate with a muffled thud, his gaze darting around the dim basement as if expecting someone to materialize.
